followup_notes.changes_from_prior_cycle: I keep the verdict as promising but slightly lower the overall score from 7.0 to 6.8. The other reviews reinforced the analogue-versus-test framing issue and the photometric limitation. I also soften my earlier concern that the 'virtual speed of light' calculation is only partially supported: the calculation itself is well supported, though the wording remains visualization-oriented.
invalid_points_from_other_reviewers: I do not accept the strongest version of the claim that the result is mathematically guaranteed and therefore has little experimental value; the apparatus still demonstrates a nontrivial physical reconstruction and validates the practical imaging method within its analogue scope.

I do not accept the assertion that the methodology is 'perfectly sound' at the 9/10 technical level from the main-text bundle alone; the missing quantitative error budget, unavailable code/data, and finite-gate effects justify a lower technical score.
The 'real time' wording is imprecise, but I would not treat it as a substantive contradiction if it refers to playback/slow-motion visualization rather than live capture.
valid_points_from_other_reviewers: The objection that the experiment encodes the equal-arrival-time rule into the construction is valid as an epistemic limitation, though it does not invalidate the analogue demonstration.
The finite gate width versus slice spacing concern is valid and should be included as a resolution/error-budget issue.
The point that per-slice normalization removes physically meaningful intensity information is valid; the experiment demonstrates geometry, not full relativistic photometry.
The 11% sphere elongation artifact deserves explicit attention, especially because the sphere lacks a simulation overlay comparable to the cube.
The finite-distance/non-parallel-ray distortion treatment for the cube is a genuine strength rather than merely an unexplained flaw.

main_claims.claim: In an ideal snapshot, Lorentz contraction of a moving 3D object is not directly visible; under parallel-ray conditions the object appears as the rest object rotated, with a sphere still appearing spherical.
main_claims.notes: This is the standard Penrose/Terrell result and is explained clearly by the equal-arrival-time argument. I am not certifying the supplementary derivation, which is not included in the bundle.

main_claims.claim: The experiment/lab analogue synthesizes snapshots of relativistically moving objects using picosecond illumination, gated imaging, pre-contracted models, object repositioning, and diagonal recombination of time/position slices.
main_claims.notes: The method is described coherently. Its status should be framed as an analogue reconstruction of the geometric snapshot condition rather than direct photography of real relativistic motion.
main_claims.paper_location: Introduction final paragraph; Methods/Setup; Fig. 4; Eq. (2)

main_claims.claim: The cube and sphere synthesized snapshots qualitatively demonstrate visual compensation of Lorentz contraction and apparent rotation.
main_claims.notes: The visual agreement is persuasive, especially for the cube with simulation overlay. The claim is limited by finite gate width, thresholding/normalization, the 11% sphere elongation artifact, and limited quantitative residual analysis in the main text.

main_claims.claim: The setup achieves a virtual light-speed scale of about 1.8 m/s in playback.
main_claims.notes: The arithmetic follows from 6 cm slice spacing at 30 fps. This is a visualization scale, not a physical change in c.

main_claims.claim: This is the first experimental visualization/test of the Terrell-Penrose effect.
main_claims.notes: The claim is plausible as a first macroscopic lab analogue visualization, but the stronger wording 'test' needs literature qualification and should distinguish analogue visualization from independent empirical testing of relativistic kinematics.

missing_assumptions.assumption: Sequentially sampled, repositioned static objects are optically equivalent to a continuously moving relativistic object for the geometric snapshot condition.
missing_assumptions.why_it_matters: This is the central analogue step. It requires repeatability, correct displacement calibration, stable scattering/illumination, and a valid mapping from optical path length to simulated emission time.

missing_assumptions.assumption: Finite gate duration, finite inter-slice spacing, and post-processing do not materially shift apparent contours.
missing_assumptions.why_it_matters: The result is visual and contour-based; gate overlap, thresholding, and per-slice normalization can alter edge visibility and apparent geometry unless bounded.

missing_assumptions.assumption: Artificially Lorentz-contracted physical models faithfully represent the instantaneous contracted geometry being simulated.
missing_assumptions.why_it_matters: For the 0.999c sphere especially, small fabrication, tilt, or alignment errors can be visually amplified; the paper already reports an 11% elongation artifact.

missing_assumptions.assumption: Source-camera offset, near-field/spherical-wave illumination, and finite observer distance are fully captured by the simulations used for comparison.
missing_assumptions.paper_location: Setup; Results discussion of Fig. 5c; Fig. 6
missing_assumptions.why_it_matters: The paper notes duplicated edges and hyperbolic distortions when the parallel-ray idealization fails. A rigorous comparison depends on the fidelity of this non-ideal optical model.

missing_assumptions.assumption: The novelty claim excludes prior analogue, stroboscopic, computational-with-physical-display, or indirect experimental demonstrations.
missing_assumptions.why_it_matters: The phrase 'never been tested experimentally' is stronger than 'first macroscopic lab analogue visualization' and needs a literature boundary.

needs_human_spotcheck.question: Do the supplementary methods derive Eq. (2) and the apparent rotation under the actual source-camera geometry, finite gate width, finite slice spacing, and finite observer distance?
needs_human_spotcheck.why_model_cannot_resolve: The provided bundle references supplementary methods but does not include them, and the main text gives only a qualitative account of several non-idealities.

needs_human_spotcheck.question: Is the 'first experimental visualization/test' claim accurate against the broader literature on Terrell rotation, relativistic optics, stroboscopic demonstrations, and ultrafast imaging analogues?
needs_human_spotcheck.why_model_cannot_resolve: Absence of prior work requires a targeted literature review beyond the cited references and supplied text.

needs_human_spotcheck.question: Do raw or minimally processed images quantitatively match simulations before thresholding and per-slice normalization?
needs_human_spotcheck.why_model_cannot_resolve: The data and code are available only on request, so the bundle does not permit independent reproduction or residual analysis.

needs_human_spotcheck.question: Is the 11% sphere elongation fully explained by the reported tilt and fabrication geometry?
needs_human_spotcheck.why_model_cannot_resolve: This needs the actual model geometry, alignment parameters, and a corrected simulation or uncertainty analysis not present in the main text.

novelty_assessment: The underlying physics is classic and already extensively simulated, so the novelty is not theoretical. The contribution is a creative and technically nontrivial experimental visualization: using SEEC-style picosecond gated imaging, pre-contracted macroscopic models, mechanical displacement, and diagonal reconstruction to make Terrell-Penrose rotation visible in the lab. I regard that as more than routine pedagogy, but less than a field-shaping physics advance or independent test of special relativity.
score_rationale: The work is clear, inventive, and likely valuable as a macroscopic laboratory visualization of a classic relativistic-optics result. I lower technical soundness relative to an ideal paper because the central reconstruction is synthetic, its real-snapshot equivalence is not fully quantified in the main text, photometric relativistic effects are outside scope, and the sphere artifact is nontrivial. I still rate it promising because the experimental implementation and finite-distance discussion are meaningful and the main qualitative shape result appears credible.
summary: The paper is best read as a clever laboratory analogue and visualization of Terrell-Penrose apparent rotation, not as an independent experimental test of relativistic motion. The core geometrical construction is plausible and aligns with established theory, and the treatment of finite-distance distortions is a strength. My view after reading the other reviews is slightly more conservative on epistemic framing and photometric realism, while I reject the stronger claim that the methodology is therefore technically weak or merely circular in a way that nullifies the contribution.

technical_objections.evidence: The authors explicitly pre-contract the physical objects, displace them by the theoretically derived rule Δx = βΔz, and combine slices diagonally according to Eq. (2). This implements the equal-arrival-time geometry rather than observing an actually relativistically moving object.
technical_objections.objection: The paper sometimes blurs the distinction between an experimentally synthesized analogue visualization and an independent experimental test of relativistic motion.
technical_objections.paper_location: Abstract; Introduction final paragraph; Methods/Setup; Conclusion

technical_objections.evidence: The setup uses finite 300 ps gates, 400 ps inter-slice delays, a nearby source/camera geometry, and non-parallel illumination. The paper notes finite-distance distortions such as duplicated faces and hyperbolic vertical edges, but the main text does not give a compact error budget for how these factors affect reconstructed contours.
technical_objections.objection: The equivalence between the diagonal recombination formula and a real continuously moving optical snapshot is under-quantified in the main text for the actual experimental conditions.
technical_objections.paper_location: Methods/Setup; Eq. (2); Fig. 4; Results discussion of Fig. 5c

technical_objections.evidence: The paper normalizes all contributing slices to 1 and thresholds low-signal pixels. A true relativistic visual appearance can include Doppler, aberration, and beaming/intensity effects; these are not reproduced by static reflected-light slices and are further suppressed by normalization.
technical_objections.objection: The experiment demonstrates geometric shape/rotation but not the full visual appearance of a relativistically moving luminous or reflecting body.

technical_objections.evidence: The authors state that the horizontal north-south axis is 11% longer than the vertical equatorial diameter and attribute this to the necessary tilt of the contracted sphere for illumination. No comparable corrected overlay is shown for the sphere in the main text.
technical_objections.objection: The sphere result contains a non-negligible reported 11% elongation artifact, with less quantitative correction than the cube result.

technical_objections.evidence: The camera gate is 300 ps while consecutive delay slices are separated by 400 ps; the corresponding light-travel scale is a substantial fraction of the 6 cm effective depth spacing after the round-trip correction. The main text does not quantify the resulting contour uncertainty.
technical_objections.objection: Finite gate width is comparable to the effective slice spacing and may blur adjacent depth slices.

technical_objections.evidence: The text reports 32 timing slices, 32 object positions, 17 slices per frame, and 49 animation frames, but does not spell out the indexing/boundary convention that yields these numbers.
technical_objections.objection: The frame-count and boundary convention in the reconstruction are not fully transparent from the main text.

technical_objections.evidence: The text calls β = v/c the 'relativistic factor'; conventionally β is dimensionless velocity and γ is the Lorentz factor. The equations themselves are not harmed.
technical_objections.objection: Terminology around β is slightly imprecise.
